title: Evolution
subtitle: Origin of life, evidence, natural selection, Hardy-Weinberg principle, adaptive radiation and human evolution - made visual for NEET.

heroModel.title: Evolutionary tree
heroModel.subtitle: Drag to rotate - scroll to zoom

roadmapTitle: Evolution connects variation, selection, time and ancestry.
roadmapDescription: Read the chapter as a story: life arose, evidence supports change, selection shifts populations, and lineages branch across time.
flowchart:
flowchart LR
  A[Origin of life] --> B[Evidence]
  B --> C[Variation]
  C --> D[Natural selection]
  D --> E[Hardy Weinberg]
  E --> F[Adaptive radiation]
  F --> G[Speciation]
  G --> H[Human evolution]

roadmap.title: Origin of life
roadmap.summary: Chemical evolution explains formation of early organic molecules.

roadmap.title: Evidence for evolution
roadmap.summary: Fossils, comparative anatomy, embryology and biochemistry support evolution.

roadmap.title: Darwinian selection
roadmap.summary: Heritable variations with survival advantage become common.

roadmap.title: Hardy-Weinberg principle
roadmap.summary: Allele frequencies remain stable unless evolutionary forces act.

roadmap.title: Adaptive radiation
roadmap.summary: One ancestor gives rise to many forms in different niches.

roadmap.title: Speciation
roadmap.summary: Isolation and accumulated changes create new species.

roadmap.title: Human evolution
roadmap.summary: Primates to hominins to modern humans through a fossil sequence.

roadmap.title: Modern view
roadmap.summary: Mutation, recombination, gene flow, drift and selection alter populations.

conceptDescription: These cards shift the chapter from story memorisation to cause-effect clarity.

sections.title: Chemical evolution and early life
sections.description: Life is thought to have arisen from non-living organic molecules under primitive Earth conditions.
points: Primitive atmosphere was reducing.
Oparin and Haldane proposed chemical evolution.
Miller-Urey experiment produced amino acids under simulated early conditions.
First life forms were likely simple anaerobic heterotrophs.
sections.neet: Miller-Urey experiment is the classic origin-of-life experiment.

sections.title: Evidence for evolution
sections.description: Multiple independent observations support descent with modification.
points: Fossils show life forms from different geological periods.
Homologous organs show divergent evolution.
Analogous organs show convergent evolution.
Molecular similarity supports common ancestry.
sections.neet: Homology indicates common ancestry; analogy indicates similar function, not same origin.

sections.title: Natural selection
sections.description: Natural selection favours heritable variations that increase survival or reproduction.
points: Variation exists in populations.
Organisms overproduce offspring.
Competition occurs for resources.
Favourable variations are selected over generations.
sections.neet: Natural selection acts on phenotype but changes allele frequencies.

sections.title: Hardy-Weinberg principle
sections.description: A non-evolving population maintains allele frequencies under ideal conditions.
points: Equation is p squared + 2pq + q squared = 1.
p and q are allele frequencies.
Mutation, migration, drift, recombination and selection disturb equilibrium.
Genetic drift is strong in small populations.
sections.neet: Hardy-Weinberg is a null model for detecting evolution.

sections.title: Adaptive radiation
sections.description: Different forms evolve from a common ancestor in different habitats or niches.
points: Darwin finches show adaptive radiation.
Australian marsupials show adaptive radiation.
Convergent evolution can produce similar adaptations in unrelated groups.
Ecological opportunity speeds diversification.
sections.neet: Darwin finches are a textbook adaptive radiation example.

sections.title: Isolation and species formation
sections.description: New species form when gene flow is restricted and differences accumulate.
points: Geographical isolation can separate populations.
Reproductive isolation prevents interbreeding.
Mutation and selection build differences.
Speciation is usually gradual across many generations.
sections.neet: Isolation plus accumulated genetic change can form new species.

sections.title: Human evolution
sections.description: Human evolution is reconstructed using fossils and comparative features.
points: Australopithecus was more ape-like but walked upright.
Homo habilis used tools.
Homo erectus had larger brain and used fire.
Homo sapiens shows modern cranial features and culture.
sections.neet: Human evolution is branching, not a straight ladder.

sections.title: Evolutionary forces
sections.description: Several mechanisms alter allele frequencies in populations.
points: Mutation creates new variation.
Recombination reshuffles variation.
Gene flow moves alleles between populations.
Natural selection and drift change allele frequencies.
sections.neet: Evolution is change in allele frequency over generations.

modelDescription: Original models represent branching ancestry, population curves, selection and variation.
highYield: Fossils are direct evidence of past life.
Hardy-Weinberg equation is p squared + 2pq + q squared = 1.
Genetic drift is chance change in small populations.
Gene flow is allele movement between populations.
Human evolution is branching, not linear.

mistakes.title: Homology vs analogy
mistakes.body: Homology means common origin; analogy means similar function due to convergence.

mistakes.title: Hardy-Weinberg meaning
mistakes.body: It describes no evolution under ideal conditions, not automatic change.

quiz.question: Miller-Urey experiment produced:
quiz.explain: The experiment produced simple organic molecules including amino acids.

quiz.question: Analogous organs indicate:
quiz.explain: Analogous organs have similar function but different origin.

quiz.question: Hardy-Weinberg equation is:
quiz.explain: It represents genotype frequencies for two alleles.

quiz.question: Genetic drift is strongest in:
quiz.explain: Chance effects are stronger in small populations.

quiz.question: Speciation often requires:
quiz.explain: Isolation reduces gene flow and differences accumulate.

quiz.question: Homo habilis is associated with:
quiz.explain: Homo habilis means handy man and is linked with tools.

flashcards.back: Many forms from one ancestor in different niches.
